---
title: "1.8 Documentation with Comments and Preconditions"
subtitle: "Part 1: Comments and Documentation Comments"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Comments are written for programmers

Adding comments to your code helps to make it more readable and maintainable.

In the commercial world, software development is usually a team effort where many programmers will use your code and maintain it for years.

Comments are written for both the original programmer and other programmers to understand the code and its functionality, but are ignored by the compiler and are not executed when the program is run.

## Comment Types

### Three types in Java

There are 3 types of comments in Java:

::: {.tight-list}
1. `//` Single line comment
2. `/*` Multiline block of comments `*/`
3. `/**` Java documentation comments `*/`
:::

The special characters `//` mark the rest of the line as a comment.

For multiple lines, use `/*` to start the comment and `*/` to end the comment.

## Documentation Comments

### `/** */`

There is also a special version of the multi-line comment, `/** */`, called the <span class="term">documentation comment</span> (文档注释).

Java has a tool called `javadoc` that will pull out these comments to make documentation of a class as a web page.

Although you do not have to use this in the AP exam, it is a good idea to use documentation comments in front of classes, methods, and instance variables in case you want to save your code as a library.

## Comment Match

### `dragndrop:: comments`

Drag the definition from the left and drop it on the correct symbols on the right.

| Definition | Symbol |
|---|---|
| single-line comment |  |
| multi-line comment |  |
| Java documentation comment |  |

Symbols: `//`, `/* */`, `/** */`.

## Comment Match Answer

### `comments`

| Definition | Symbol |
|---|---|
| single-line comment | `//` |
| multi-line comment | `/* */` |
| Java documentation comment | `/** */` |

## Comments Do Not Run

### Compiler behavior

The compiler will skip over comments, and they do not affect how your program runs.

They are for you, your teacher, and other programmers working with you.

Most IDEs tend to show comments formatted in italics to make them easier to spot.

## Comment Example {.fit-small}

### Textbook example

```java
/**
* MyClass.java
* @author My Name
* @since Date
* This class keeps track of the max score.
*/
public class MyClass()
{
   private int max = 10; // this keeps track of the max score
   /* The print() method prints out the max */
   public print()
   {
      System.out.println(max);
   }
}
```

## Documentation Tags

### Common Javadoc tags

Notice that there are some special tags that you can use in Java documentation.

These are not required, but many programmers use them.

::: {.tight-list}
- `@author` Author of the program
- `@since` Date released
- `@version` Version of program
- `@param` Parameter of a method
- `@return` Return value for a method
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: commentExercise`

The code below will read in the `2` numbers below it and multiply them.

Add a multiline comment above the `public class` to describe what the class does.

Add single-line comments above each section of the code to read in, calculate, and print the result to describe what it does.

Input used by Runestone: `5 5`.

## Program to Annotate

### `commentExercise`

```java
import java.util.Scanner;




 public class Multiply
 {
      public static void main(String[] args)
      {
           Scanner scan = new Scanner(System.in);
           int num1 = scan.nextInt();
           int num2 = scan.nextInt();

           int result = num1 * num2;

           System.out.println(num1 + " x " + num2 + " = " + result);
           scan.close();
      }
 }
```

## Test Requirements

### `commentExercise`

Runestone checks the submitted code with comments.

::: {.tight-list}
- the code contains `/*`
- the code contains `//`
:::

The checks look for both multiline and single-line comments.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain that comments are ignored by the compiler and not executed
- identify `//`, `/* */`, and `/** */`
- distinguish documentation comments from ordinary multiline comments
- match common Javadoc tags to their purpose
- add a multiline class comment for `Multiply`
- add single-line comments for reading input, calculating, and printing
:::

## End

### Continue to preconditions

The next deck covers preconditions, postconditions, and code that breaks method assumptions.
